abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a popular training paradigm in the LLM community. This paradigm selects a larger model as the teacher to provide dense, fine-grained signals for each sampled trajectory, in contrast to reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which only obtains sparse signals from verifiable outcomes in the environment. Recently, the community has explored on-policy self-distillation (OPSD), where the same model serves as both teacher and student, with the teacher receiving additional privileged information such as reference answers to enable self-evolution. This paper demonstrates that learning signals solely derived from the privileged teacher result in severe information leakage and unstable long-term training. Accordingly, we identify the optimal niche for self-distillation and propose \textbf{RLSD} (\textbf{RL}VR with \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{D}istillation). Specifically, we leverage self-distillation to obtain token-level policy differences for determining fine-grained update magnitudes, while continuing to use RLVR to derive reliable update directions from environmental feedback (e.g., response correctness). This enables RLSD to simultaneously harness the strengths of both RLVR and OPSD, achieving a higher convergence ceiling and superior training stability.

citations.fullCitation: Zhihong Shao, Peiyi Wang, Qihao Zhu, Runxin Xu, Junxiao Song, Xiao Bi, Haowei Zhang, Mingchuan Zhang, Y. K. Li, Y. Wu, and Daya Guo. Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.03300.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which is the primary Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) baseline method used throughout the paper. The proposed RLSD method is presented as a direct enhancement to the GRPO framework, replacing its uniform sequence-level advantage with a fine-grained, token-level credit assignment mechanism.
citations.title: Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

citations.fullCitation: Rishabh Agarwal, Nino Vieillard, Yongchao Zhou, Piotr Stanczyk, Sabela Ramos Garea, Matthieu Geist, and Olivier Bachem. On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes. In The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations, 2024. URL https://openreview.net/forum?id=3zKtaqxLhW.
citations.justification: This work defines On-Policy Distillation (OPD), a key training paradigm that the paper contrasts with On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD). The central question motivating the research—"why does OPD work while OPSD fails?"—makes this citation fundamental to understanding the paper's core theoretical analysis.
citations.title: On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes

citations.fullCitation: Siyan Zhao, Zhihui Xie, Mengchen Liu, Jing Huang, Guan Pang, Feiyu Chen, and Aditya Grover. Self-distilled reasoner: On-policy self-distillation for large language models, 2026. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18734.
citations.justification: This paper introduces On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), the primary method that the main paper analyzes, critiques, and uses as a baseline. The theoretical and empirical analysis of OPSD's failure due to information leakage forms the central motivation for the development of the proposed RLSD method.
citations.title: Self-distilled reasoner: On-policy self-distillation for large language models

citations.fullCitation: John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, and Oleg Klimov. Proximal policy optimization algorithms, 2017. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a foundational policy gradient algorithm in reinforcement learning. The main paper's baseline, GRPO, is a variant of PPO, and the proposed RLSD method explicitly adopts PPO's design philosophy of using a clipped objective to stabilize training, applying it to token-level credit assignment.
citations.title: Proximal policy optimization algorithms

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Self-Distilled RLVR

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "Self-Distilled RLVR" was authored by Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, Qingyi Si, Minghui Chen, Naibin Gu, Dingyu Yao, Zheng Lin, Weiping Wang, Jiaqi Wang, and Nan Duan.

The affiliations of the authors are:
*   **Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China, and School of Cyber Security, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China:** Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, Minghui Chen, Naibin Gu, Dingyu Yao, Zheng Lin, Weiping Wang.
*   **JD.COM:** Qingyi Si, Jiaqi Wang, Nan Duan.

Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, and Qingyi Si contributed equally to this work. Zheng Lin is the corresponding author.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The field of Large Language Model (LLM) post-training for complex reasoning tasks is broadly characterized by two main paradigms: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and On-Policy Distillation (OPD). RLVR methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), typically utilize sparse, scalar rewards obtained from environmental feedback (e.g., correctness of an answer) to guide model updates. While RLVR provides reliable directional signals for learning, the sparsity of these signals can limit the granularity of credit assignment to individual tokens within a generated sequence.

In contrast, OPD aims to provide dense, token-level supervision by leveraging a separate, often larger, teacher model to evaluate the student model's on-policy trajectories. This approach can lead to faster convergence due to the rich feedback, but it incurs substantial computational overhead and requires the teacher and student models to share a common vocabulary, limiting scalability.

A more recent development is On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), which attempts to overcome the limitations of OPD by using the same model as both teacher and student. In OPSD, the "teacher" variant of the model is conditioned on privileged information, such as verified reasoning traces, to provide dense token-level signals, thereby eliminating the need for an external teacher model and improving token efficiency. However, the present work identifies fundamental limitations within the OPSD paradigm, including issues of information leakage and training instability.

This paper positions itself by critically analyzing the shortcomings of OPSD and proposing a novel framework, RLVR with Self-Distillation (RLSD), that aims to synthesize the strengths of RLVR's reliable environmental anchoring and OPSD's token-level efficiency, without incurring their respective drawbacks. It seeks to refine the post-training landscape by offering a more stable and effective method for fine-grained credit assignment in reasoning LLMs.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary motivation for this research stems from observed inconsistencies and theoretical vulnerabilities in the On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) paradigm, despite its promise of efficient, dense supervision. While OPSD offers the advantage of not requiring an external teacher model and providing rich, token-level learning signals, empirical observations revealed that OPSD-trained models exhibit a pattern of early performance peak followed by subsequent degradation. This decline is accompanied by a phenomenon termed "privileged information leakage," where the model generates outputs that explicitly reference information it was only exposed to during the teacher's privileged operation, but which is unavailable at inference time. These issues challenge the long-term effectiveness and reliability of OPSD.

Against this backdrop, the key objectives of this work are articulated as follows:

1.  **Identify the Root Cause of OPSD's Failure**: The first objective is to rigorously investigate and formally explain why OPSD leads to privileged information leakage and unstable training dynamics. This involves both empirical diagnostic experiments and a theoretical analysis of the underlying objective function and gradient dynamics. The authors aim to demonstrate that the information asymmetry inherent in OPSD, where the teacher conditions on privileged information (e.g., a verified reasoning trace `r`) that the student cannot access during inference, creates a fundamental mismatch that renders the distribution-matching objective ill-posed.

2.  **Propose a New Training Paradigm (RLSD)**: Based on the identified root cause, the second objective is to design and develop a novel training paradigm, RLVR with Self-Distillation (RLSD). The core principle behind RLSD is to decouple the functions of update direction and update magnitude. It is motivated by the insight that reliable update directions are best derived from sparse but verifiable environmental feedback (like in RLVR), while fine-grained update magnitudes can benefit from dense, token-level signals (like in self-distillation). RLSD proposes to repurpose the privileged teacher's signal from a generative target to a magnitude evaluator within a policy gradient framework.

3.  **Demonstrate Superior Performance and Stability**: The final objective is to empirically validate that RLSD overcomes the limitations of OPSD and achieves improved performance and training stability. This includes demonstrating that RLSD reaches a higher convergence ceiling and maintains consistent improvement without the degradation observed in OPSD, particularly across challenging multimodal reasoning benchmarks. Furthermore, the authors aim to show that RLSD achieves these benefits efficiently, without the need for auxiliary networks or significant computational overhead beyond standard RLVR methods.

By addressing these objectives, the research aims to provide a robust and theoretically grounded method for leveraging self-distillation in LLM post-training, thus advancing the state of the art in developing reasoning models.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology employed in this research comprises two main phases: a detailed analysis of OPSD's failure modes and the subsequent design and implementation of the proposed RLSD framework.

**Analysis of OPSD's Failure:**
The authors initiated their investigation by conducting empirical diagnostics on OPSD-trained models. They observed and quantified:
*   **Privileged Information Leakage**: The frequency of models explicitly referencing unseen "reference solutions" during inference was tracked, showing a monotonic increase over training steps.
*   **Performance Degradation**: Validation accuracy was monitored, revealing an initial peak within the first 10-20 steps, followed by a decline, correlating with increased leakage.
*   **KL Divergence Stagnation**: The on-policy KL divergence between teacher and student distributions under OPSD showed an initial drop but then plateaued, indicating a lack of sustained convergence. In contrast, OPD showed a steady decrease.

To explain these empirical observations, the authors developed a theoretical framework:
*   **Ill-Posed Objective**: They argued that OPSD's objective, which forces a student model that cannot condition on privileged information `r` to match a teacher model that does, is fundamentally ill-posed.
*   **Theorem 1 (KL Decomposition)**: This theorem formally establishes that the OPSD objective contains an irreducible conditional mutual information gap, `I(Y_t; R | X, Y_<t>)`, between the current token `Y_t` and the privileged information `R`. This gap is independent of the student's parameters (θ) and cannot be eliminated, mathematically explaining the observed KL stagnation.
*   **Gradient Structure Analysis (Proposition 1)**: While the *expected* OPSD gradient is theoretically benign, the authors decomposed the per-sample gradient into a beneficial marginal-matching component and an `r`-specific deviation component `δ(θ; r)`. They demonstrated that the variance of this deviation component is proportional to the irreducible mutual information gap.
*   **Two-Phase Training Dynamics**: This analysis led to the hypothesis of a two-phase training dynamic: early in training, the beneficial component dominates, leading to rapid improvements. As the student approaches the teacher's marginal distribution, the beneficial component diminishes, and the `r`-specific deviation component, which does not decay, increasingly dominates parameter updates. This drives the model to encode spurious correlations between input `x` and privileged information `r`, leading to leakage and degradation.
*   **Leakage Bandwidth Experiments**: Controlled experiments with ablated OPSD variants (Teacher's Top-1, Student's Top-1) were performed. These variants, which compress the distillation target differently, still exhibited leakage, confirming that any mechanism where the teacher's `r`-dependent evaluation enters the gradient direction leads to leakage. The severity of leakage correlated with the "bandwidth" of `r`-specific information influencing the gradient.
*   **Impossibility Trilemma (Appendix A.4)**: An extended analysis in the appendix establishes that under shared parameters, OPSD cannot simultaneously achieve objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training.

**RLSD: Reinforcement Learning with Self-Distillation:**
Based on the identified limitations, the authors proposed RLSD, which reconfigures the role of self-distillation. Instead of distribution matching, RLSD uses the teacher's privileged signal for fine-grained *credit assignment magnitude*, while preserving the environment reward for *update direction*. The approach consists of three steps:

1.  **Privileged Information Gain (Δ_t)**: For each token `y_t` in a student-sampled trajectory, `Δ_t` is computed as `sg(log P_T(y_t | x, r, y_<t>) - log P_S(y_t | x, y_<t>))`. The `stop-gradient (sg)` operator ensures `Δ_t` acts purely as a weighting signal, isolating the marginal contribution of `r` to the prediction of `y_t`.
2.  **Direction-Aware Evidence Reweighting (w_t)**: A per-token weight `w_t = exp(sign(A) · Δ_t)` is constructed. `A` is the sequence-level advantage from the environment verifier. `sign(A)` ensures that if the trajectory is correct (A > 0), tokens supported by `r` receive increased weight (`P_T/P_S`). If incorrect (A < 0), tokens disfavored by `r` receive increased blame (`P_S/P_T`). This provides a Bayesian interpretation where `w_t` quantifies the sequential belief update in `r` after observing `y_t`.
3.  **Clipped Credit Assignment (Â_t)**: The final token-level advantage `Â_t = A · ((1 - λ) + λ · clip(w_t, 1 - ϵ_w, 1 + ϵ_w))` is computed. This clips `w_t` to stabilize training and introduces a mixing coefficient `λ` to linearly interpolate between uniform advantage and reweighted advantage, decaying `λ` over training steps to gradually transition towards vanilla GRPO.

**Integration and Guarantees**: `Â_t` is designed as a drop-in replacement for the uniform advantage in the standard GRPO objective, incurring only one additional forward pass per response. The authors formally demonstrate that RLSD provides a leakage-free guarantee by ensuring that the privileged information `r` influences only the magnitude of credit, not the gradient direction or the set of tokens receiving gradient signal. This architectural design also allows RLSD to circumvent the impossibility trilemma, simultaneously achieving objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research yielded a set of findings concerning the mechanisms of OPSD failure and the performance characteristics of the proposed RLSD method.

**Findings on OPSD Failure:**
*   **Empirical Leakage and Degradation**: Controlled experiments demonstrated that OPSD-trained models consistently exhibit privileged information leakage, where the model incorporates references to unseen private information (e.g., "reference solution") into its outputs. This leakage progressively intensified with training, correlating directly with a degradation in validation accuracy after an initial period of improvement.
*   **KL Divergence Stagnation**: Under OPSD, the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the teacher and student distributions quickly plateaued, failing to show sustained reduction. This suggested an inherent barrier to convergence, unlike the continuous reduction observed in traditional On-Policy Distillation (OPD).
*   **Theoretical Explanation of Ill-Posed Objective**: The authors theoretically established that OPSD's distribution-matching objective is fundamentally ill-posed due to an irreducible conditional mutual information gap, `I(Y_t; R | X, Y_<t>)`, between the current token and the privileged information. This gap, being independent of model parameters, prevents the student from fully matching the teacher's distribution and acts as a non-reducible lower bound on the loss.
*   **Gradient-Level Pathology**: Analysis of per-sample gradients in OPSD revealed that, while the *expected* gradient is benign, individual gradients contain an `r`-specific deviation component. The variance of this deviation is proportional to the mutual information gap, indicating that privileged information actively corrupts parameter updates, steering them towards encoding spurious correlations between the input and the privileged information (i.e., leakage). This explains the observed two-phase training dynamics.
*   **Universality of Leakage**: Experiments with various ablated OPSD variants confirmed that any formulation where the teacher's privileged evaluation directly influences the gradient direction leads to information leakage, with severity depending on the "leakage bandwidth" or how widely `r`-specific information influences token probabilities.

**Results of RLSD Performance:**
*   **Superior Average Accuracy**: RLSD achieved the highest average accuracy across five multimodal reasoning benchmarks (MMMU, MathVista, MathVision, ZeroBench, WeMath). It surpassed the Base LLM by 4.69% and standard GRPO by 2.32% on average.
*   **Enhanced Reasoning in Mathematical Tasks**: RLSD demonstrated notable performance gains on complex mathematical reasoning datasets, such as MathVista (+1.9% relative to GRPO) and MathVision (+3.91% relative to GRPO). This is attributed to its ability to provide fine-grained, token-level credit assignment, which is particularly beneficial for step-by-step reasoning problems.
*   **Outperformance of Self-Distillation Baselines**: RLSD consistently outperformed OPSD and SDPO, supporting the theoretical conclusion that directly matching a privileged teacher's distribution is problematic under information asymmetry.
*   **Improved over Combined Approaches**: RLSD also exceeded the performance of GRPO+OPSD, a baseline that linearly combines reinforcement learning and distillation losses. This suggests that RLSD's multiplicative modulation of advantage, which guarantees sign preservation, is a more effective integration strategy than additive loss combination.
*   **Stable Training Dynamics**: During training, RLSD exhibited a steeper initial performance ascent and converged to a higher accuracy ceiling compared to GRPO, without the late-stage performance collapse observed in OPSD.
*   **Higher Entropy Maintenance**: RLSD maintained consistently higher entropy levels than GRPO, indicating better exploration and avoiding the premature convergence issues associated with uniform sequence-level rewards.
*   **Active Clipping Mechanism**: The credit assignment clipping mechanism in RLSD was actively engaged, with clip ratios stabilizing between 3%–6%, confirming its role in bounding per-token influence and ensuring training stability.
*   **Qualitative Credit Assignment**: Case studies illustrated that RLSD effectively assigned credit at the token level: concentrating higher credit on decisive reasoning steps in correct trajectories and stronger blame on erroneous steps in incorrect ones, aligning with its design goal of targeted, verifier-grounded credit assignment.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research contributes to the field of Large Language Model (LLM) post-training in several significant ways, with potential implications for the development of more robust and capable reasoning systems.

**Theoretical Advancements:**
The work provides a detailed theoretical explanation for the observed failures of On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), specifically the phenomena of privileged information leakage and performance degradation. By formally identifying an irreducible mutual information gap within OPSD's distribution-matching objective, the authors reveal a fundamental structural limitation. This analysis, which extends to the gradient level and identifies problematic per-sample deviations, deepens the understanding of self-distillation in information-asymmetric settings. The formulation of an "impossibility trilemma" under shared parameters further highlights the inherent trade-offs in existing self-distillation approaches. These theoretical insights are crucial for guiding future research in knowledge distillation and reinforcement learning for LLMs.

**Methodological Innovation:**
RLSD introduces a novel approach to combining the strengths of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and self-distillation. By repurposing the privileged teacher's signal to modulate only the *magnitude* of token-level credit, while anchoring the *direction* of updates to reliable environmental feedback, RLSD circumvents the issues of information leakage and instability that plague OPSD. This decoupling strategy represents a significant methodological advance, offering a principled way to achieve fine-grained credit assignment without compromising training integrity.

**Practical Impact and Efficiency:**
*   **Improved Performance and Stability**: The empirical results demonstrate that RLSD achieves superior average accuracy and training stability across various challenging multimodal reasoning benchmarks. This suggests that RLSD can lead to more effective and reliable post-training for LLMs.
*   **Computational Efficiency**: A key practical benefit of RLSD is its computational efficiency. It operates as a self-distillation method, eliminating the need for a separate, often larger, external teacher model required by traditional On-Policy Distillation (OPD). The additional computational cost is minimal, amounting to a single extra forward pass per response to obtain teacher logits, which is negligible compared to the overall training pipeline.
*   **Ease of Integration**: RLSD is designed as a direct "drop-in replacement" for the uniform advantage in standard GRPO pipelines. This ease of integration means that practitioners can adopt RLSD to enhance their existing RLVR workflows without requiring substantial architectural changes or the introduction of complex auxiliary losses or networks.
*   **Enhanced Reasoning Abilities**: The gains observed in mathematical and complex reasoning tasks indicate that RLSD's fine-grained credit assignment mechanism can effectively guide LLMs to learn more precise and robust reasoning steps, potentially leading to advancements in areas requiring multi-step logical inference.

In conclusion, this work offers both a critical diagnostic tool for understanding the pitfalls of certain self-distillation methods and a robust, efficient, and theoretically sound alternative. By enabling LLMs to learn more effectively from both verifiable outcomes and self-generated privileged insights, RLSD has the potential to accelerate the development of more capable and trustworthy AI agents for complex reasoning tasks.
overview:
## The Post-Training Landscape of Reasoning Models

Post-training large language models (LLMs) for complex reasoning tasks typically follows one of two paths. The first is Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). These methods use outcome-based feedback—checking if a math answer is correct or if code passes a test—to provide a reliable learning signal. However, these signals are often sparse; they tell the model that a long sequence of reasoning was "right" or "wrong" without specifying which individual steps were the most critical.

The second path is On-Policy Distillation (OPD), which provides dense, token-level feedback by having a larger "teacher" model evaluate every step the "student" model takes. While effective, this is computationally expensive and requires a teacher model that shares the same vocabulary. A variation of this is On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), where the model acts as its own teacher. By giving itself privileged information (like a gold-standard reasoning trace), the model creates a "teacher mode" to supervise its own "student mode."

![Performance dynamics and accuracy comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v2/x1.png)
*Figure 1: (a) Performance dynamics showing RLSD's stability versus OPSD's collapse. (b) Performance across benchmarks MMMU, MathVista, and ZeroBench.*

This research identifies a critical flaw in OPSD: "privileged information leakage." Because the teacher has access to information that the student will not have at inference time, the student begins to hallucinate or explicitly reference that hidden information during training. This leads to a performance peak followed by a sharp decline. To solve this, the authors propose Reinforcement Learning with Self-Distillation (RLSD), a framework that uses self-distillation signals to adjust the *magnitude* of reinforcement learning updates while letting environmental feedback control the *direction*.

## The Failure of On-Policy Self-Distillation

To understand why OPSD fails, we must look at the information asymmetry between its teacher and student modes. In OPSD, for a given question $x$, the model samples a response $y$. To train, it calculates a loss based on the difference between the student distribution (conditioned only on $x$) and the teacher distribution (conditioned on $x$ and a privileged reasoning trace $r$).

The researchers found that OPSD-trained models exhibit three distinct pathologies:
1.  **Information Leakage:** As training progresses, models increasingly generate phrases like "According to the reference solution..." or "The correct answer is [hidden value]," even when $r$ is not provided.
2.  **Performance Degradation:** Accuracy initially improves but then crashes.
3.  **KL Stagnation:** The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, which measures the difference between the teacher and student, stops decreasing and plateaus.

The authors prove that this failure is due to an "ill-posed objective." Mathematically, the goal of OPSD is to minimize:

$$\mathcal{L}_{OPSD}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot|x, r)} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^{|y|} D_{KL}(\pi_\theta(\cdot|x, r, y_{<t}) || \pi_\theta(\cdot|x, y_{<t})) \right]$$

However, they demonstrate that even at the theoretical optimum, the KL divergence cannot reach zero. It is bounded by the conditional mutual information between the token $y_t$ and the privileged information $r$:

$$D_{KL}(\pi_\theta(Y_t|x, r, y_{<t}) || \pi_\theta(Y_t|x, y_{<t})) \geq I(Y_t; R | X=x, Y_{<t}=y_{<t})$$

This gap represents information that the student physically cannot know without $r$. In an attempt to bridge this gap, the model's parameters $\theta$ are updated in a way that forces the student to "guess" or "hallucinate" the contents of $r$, leading to the observed leakage.

![Leakage and KL Divergence analysis](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v2/x2.png)
*Figure 2: (a) Monitoring the count of privileged information leakage. (b) Performance curves showing early peaks. (c) KL divergence comparison between OPSD and standard OPD.*

## Reinforcement Learning with Self-Distillation (RLSD)

The core innovation of RLSD is to stop using the teacher's distribution as a direct target for the student's output. Instead, RLSD uses the teacher to answer a simpler question: "Given that we know the right answer, how important was this specific token $y_t$?"

### 1. Calculating Privileged Information Gain
For every token $y_t$ in a generated sequence, the model calculates the difference in log-probabilities between its teacher mode and its student mode:

$$\Delta_t = \text{sg}(\log P_T(y_t | x, r, y_{<t}) - \log P_S(y_t | x, y_{<t}))$$

The "stop-gradient" ($\text{sg}$) operator is crucial. It ensures that $r$ only influences the weight of the update, not the direction of the gradient itself. This prevents the student from trying to mimic the teacher's internal state.

### 2. Direction-Aware Reweighting
RLSD then combines this gain with the environmental reward $A$ (the "advantage" from the verifier). It calculates a weight $w_t$:

$$w_t = \exp(\text{sign}(A) \cdot \Delta_t)$$

This weight behaves intuitively:
*   If the response was correct ($A > 0$): Tokens that the teacher strongly supports (high $\Delta_t$) get a higher weight. The model is told: "This step was particularly responsible for the right answer."
*   If the response was incorrect ($A < 0$): Tokens that the teacher supports get a *lower* weight, while tokens the teacher disfavors get penalized more heavily. The model is told: "Even though the teacher liked this token, the final answer was wrong, so be careful."

### 3. Clipped Credit Assignment
To maintain training stability, the final advantage $\hat{A}_t$ is calculated by applying a clipping mechanism and a mixing coefficient $\lambda$:

$$\hat{A}_t = A \cdot ((1 - \lambda) + \lambda \cdot \text{clip}(w_t, 1 - \epsilon_w, 1 + \epsilon_w))$$

Here, $\epsilon_w$ is a hyperparameter that limits how much any single token can influence the update. This prevents extreme weights from causing the training to diverge.

![RLSD Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v2/x3.png)
*Figure 3: The RLSD architecture showing how the privileged signal ($\Delta_t$) modulates the advantage ($A$) to produce token-level advantages ($\hat{A}_t$).*

## Experimental Results

The researchers evaluated RLSD on several difficult multimodal and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, including MMMU, MathVista, and ZeroBench. They compared RLSD against base models, standard GRPO (RLVR), and OPSD.

Across the board, RLSD consistently outperformed its counterparts. For instance, on MathVision, RLSD achieved a score of 22.31%, compared to 18.40% for GRPO and 19.34% for OPSD. On average, RLSD provided a 2.32% improvement over standard GRPO while maintaining the training stability that OPSD lacks.

### Training Stability and Entropy
One of the notable findings was the behavior of model "entropy" during training. In standard RLVR, the model's entropy often collapses quickly as it finds a single "safe" way to get the reward, leading to less creative or diverse reasoning. RLSD maintained significantly higher entropy throughout training (Figure 4b). This suggests that token-level credit assignment helps the model explore the reasoning space more effectively without getting stuck in local optima.

![Training dynamics and clip ratios](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v2/x4.png)
*Figure 4: (a) Reward dynamics. (b) Entropy loss comparison. (c) Stabilization via the clipping mechanism.*

### Qualitative Analysis of Credit Assignment
To see if RLSD was actually identifying important steps, the authors visualized the token-level advantages in correct and incorrect reasoning traces. In a correct trace for a geometry problem, RLSD assigned high credit to the specific mathematical operations that led to the final count. In an incorrect trace, it successfully identified the precise logical leap where the model misinterpreted the image, assigning that token a negative advantage (Figure 5).

![Visualization of token-level credit assignment](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v2/x5.png)
*Figure 5: Heatmaps of token-level advantages. Green indicates positive credit; red indicates blame. The model correctly identifies critical reasoning steps.*

## Significance and Future Impact

This work addresses a fundamental tension in LLM training: the trade-off between the reliability of sparse rewards and the efficiency of dense supervision. By identifying the theoretical reasons for self-distillation failure—namely the conditional mutual information gap—the authors provide a roadmap for more stable distillation techniques.

RLSD is significant because it is a "drop-in" improvement for existing RL frameworks like GRPO. It requires no external teacher model and only one additional forward pass per response, making it highly efficient. The decoupling of update direction (from the environment) and update magnitude (from the self-teacher) provides a robust way to leverage privileged information without the risk of leakage or performance collapse. As reasoning models become more complex, such fine-grained credit assignment mechanisms will likely be essential for teaching models not just to get the right answer, but to understand exactly which parts of their logic were successful.

keyInsights: OPSD's failure stems from a theoretical limitation: an irreducible conditional mutual information gap in its objective function, rendering the distribution-matching ill-posed.
Privileged information leakage and performance degradation in OPSD are caused by a two-phase training dynamic where `r`-specific deviation components in gradients corrupt parameter updates.
Decoupling credit assignment into direction (from verifiable outcomes) and magnitude (from self-distillation) allows for stable, leakage-free, and efficient fine-grained learning in LLMs.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) provides sparse, scalar rewards, limiting fine-grained credit assignment crucial for complex reasoning tasks.

On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) showed promise for dense, token-level supervision but suffered from privileged information leakage, performance degradation after initial gains, and training instability.
Traditional On-Policy Distillation (OPD) is computationally expensive, requires an external teacher model, and limits scalability due to shared vocabulary requirements.
results: RLSD achieved the highest average accuracy across five multimodal reasoning benchmarks, surpassing the Base LLM by 4.69% and standard GRPO by 2.32%.
It demonstrated superior performance and stable training dynamics compared to OPSD, avoiding performance degradation and maintaining consistently higher entropy levels.
RLSD showed notable performance gains on complex mathematical reasoning datasets like MathVista (+1.9% relative to GRPO) and MathVision (+3.91% relative to GRPO).
solution: The proposed RLSD framework decouples update direction (from sparse environmental rewards) and update magnitude (from dense, token-level self-distillation signals).
It reconfigures the privileged teacher's signal to function as a magnitude evaluator for per-token credit assignment, rather than a direct generative target.

The method computes `privileged information gain (Δ_t)` and `direction-aware evidence reweighting (w_t)` to adjust token-level advantages, then applies `clipped credit assignment (Â_t)` to stabilize training and gradually mix with uniform advantage.
summary.summary: Self-Distilled RLVR (RLSD) is a post-training framework for Large Language Models that enhances reasoning capabilities by providing fine-grained, token-level credit assignment while maintaining training stability. It achieves improved average accuracy by 4.69% over the base LLM and 2.32% over standard GRPO on multimodal reasoning benchmarks.

title: Self-Distilled RLVR
